summary: Randolph Home Store (operating as Randolph's Fine Gifts) is a curated gift and home decor boutique in Atlanta's Peachtree Battle Shopping Center, selling candles, jewelry, fine barware, gourmet food, baby gifts, kitchenware, and home accessories with a deliberate focus on American artisan, Georgia-based, and Southern-made brands. It serves Atlanta-area gift-givers and home decorators — primarily women 30–65 — who want unique, thoughtful items for life occasions over mass-produced alternatives. Revenue is one-off in-store and online retail, with seasonal repeat peaks around the holidays, Mother's Day, Valentine's Day, and graduations.

campaigns.name: Welcome & Onboarding
campaigns.objective: Activate a new subscriber toward a first store visit or purchase by reproducing the 'walking in and discovering' feeling that defines the brand.
campaigns.trigger: Newsletter signup / list join — send email 1 within seconds (welcome emails are the most-opened a brand sends; opens drop ~50% if delayed).
campaigns.segment: New subscribers; branch by occasion type or recipient type if captured at signup, otherwise route to the general edition.
campaigns.rationale: This is the single biggest gap — new subscribers currently get nothing until the next ad-hoc newsletter blast, so the warmest moment of the relationship is wasted. A welcome email is the fastest way to turn a sale-curious signup into a first-purchase customer.

emails.purpose: Warm welcome, tell the brand story, set expectations, and deliver one quick win — a look at this week's new arrivals.
emails.subject: Welcome to Randolph's — your front-row seat to Atlanta's best makers
emails.preview: The candles, barware, and finds we can't keep on the shelf — and the people behind them.
keyPoints: Warm welcome and the Randolph's story: a Peachtree Battle boutique curating American artisan, Georgia-made, and Southern-made gifts
Set expectations — occasional emails with new arrivals, maker stories, and occasion ideas; never discounty, never pushy
One quick win: a link to this week's new arrivals so they feel the 'walking in and discovering' moment online
Human close — invite a reply about the occasions they shop for, and note that staff gift-wrap in-store while you keep browsing

campaigns.name: Value Newsletter (structured)
campaigns.objective: Stay top-of-mind and drive repeat visits by building authority around makers and occasions — 80% value, 20% soft offer, never discounting.
campaigns.segment: All engaged subscribers; suppress contacts still in the welcome flow and route recently-dormant contacts to win-back instead.
campaigns.rationale: With no trial, consult, or booking motion, the newsletter is this boutique's core retention engine — but today it's ad hoc and unstructured. A repeatable maker-story + occasion-spotlight format turns it from a sale blast into the reason one-occasion buyers come back.

emails.purpose: Flagship recurring edition: lead with one maker story, tie a product cluster to a current occasion, and surface new arrivals with a single soft CTA.
emails.subject: The Georgia candlemaker behind our best-selling scent
emails.preview: Plus three new finds that make an effortless summer hostess gift.
keyPoints: Lead with one maker story — the person and craft behind a featured product (the value-first 80%)
Occasion spotlight: tie a small product cluster to a timely need (summer hostess gifts, housewarmings)
New arrivals block — 3 curated finds, each with the recipient or occasion it suits
One primary CTA (visit or shop online) and a recurring 'Meet the Maker' section that builds reading habit

campaigns.name: Seasonal Occasion & Expansion
campaigns.objective: Turn one-occasion buyers into year-round gift-occasion customers with curated gift guides timed to the brand's biggest buying windows.
campaigns.trigger: Seasonal buying window opens (e.g., holiday window ~6 weeks out); or anniversary of a prior-occasion purchase (last year's Mother's Day buyer).
campaigns.segment: Past buyers and engaged subscribers; branch by recipient type (hostess, host, kids, hard-to-buy-for) and by prior occasion purchased.
campaigns.rationale: Repeat revenue here is entirely occasion- and season-driven, so the renewal/expansion play becomes timed seasonal gift guides. The holiday window is the single largest revenue peak and the highest-leverage send of the year.

emails.purpose: Holiday gift-guide kickoff: hand shoppers a pre-curated list organized by recipient to remove decision fatigue and beat the big-box price objection.
emails.subject: Your Atlanta holiday gift list, already curated
emails.preview: Hostess, host, the kids, the one who has everything — we picked the perfect thing for each.
keyPoints: Time the send to the early-November holiday window to capture the largest seasonal revenue peak
Organize by recipient type — hostess, host, kids, hard-to-buy-for — to remove decision fatigue
Spotlight Georgia and Southern makers as the 'thoughtful, not big-box' angle that answers the price objection
Remind them of in-store gift wrapping and that early shoppers get the best of limited artisan stock

campaigns.name: Win-Back / Re-Engagement
campaigns.objective: Reactivate lapsed one-occasion buyers and sale-only subscribers, and clean the list to protect deliverability.
campaigns.trigger: Inactivity threshold — ~90 days with no opens, clicks, or purchases; or a buyer who has not returned since a single past occasion.
campaigns.segment: Dormant subscribers; separately, churned one-occasion buyers who get a message referencing what they bought before.
campaigns.rationale: The core churn risk named in the intake is one-occasion buyers and sale-only signups who never get a reason to return. A win-back flow recovers 5–15% of these and sunsets the rest before they drag down sender reputation.

emails.purpose: Warm, no-pressure re-open: acknowledge the gap, remind them of the core value, and pattern-interrupt with fresh artisan arrivals.
emails.subject: It's been a while — here's what's new at Randolph's
emails.preview: New makers, new finds, and the same thoughtful gifts you came for.
keyPoints: Open warm and low-pressure — acknowledge it's been a while since their last visit or open
Remind them of core value: unique, locally made gifts they won't find at a big-box store
Pattern interrupt with fresh artisan arrivals added since their last engagement
One easy next step to visit or shop; sets up the later sunset email for non-responders

campaigns.name: Lead-Magnet Nurture
campaigns.objective: Convert cold contacts — Instagram followers and sale-only signups — toward a first purchase using a downloadable Atlanta gift guide.
campaigns.trigger: Download of the 'Atlanta Gift Guide' (a new lead magnet to be built) or an occasion-specific guide.
campaigns.segment: Contacts who have downloaded but not yet purchased; branch the middle emails by the occasion topic of the guide they chose.
campaigns.rationale: Lowest fit of the five because retail here is impulse, not a long consideration cycle — and no real lead magnet exists yet. Building a simple gift-guide magnet gives the modest-but-engaged Instagram audience and sale-only subscribers a value-first on-ramp to a first purchase.

emails.purpose: Deliver the downloaded gift guide and frame the real problem it solves — generic gifts that feel forgettable.
emails.subject: Your Atlanta Gift Guide is here — and the trap most gift-givers fall into
emails.preview: Why the 'safe' big-box gift is the one nobody remembers.
keyPoints: Deliver the downloaded asset (the curated Atlanta gift guide) immediately
Frame the real problem: generic, mass-produced gifts feel impersonal and forgettable
Position Randolph's curation as the fix — thoughtful, artisan, occasion-ready finds without an hour on Amazon
Low-friction next step: browse the guide's picks online or visit the Peachtree Battle store
